title: PagerDuty Escalation Policy Timeout Rate — Incidents Missing Step Acknowledgements
app: PagerDuty REST API poller or Event Orchestration webhook normalizer
spl:
index=alerts sourcetype="pagerduty:incident"
| eval created_epoch=coalesce(created_at_epoch, strptime(created_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))
| eval ack_epoch=coalesce(first_acknowledgement_at_epoch, strptime(first_acknowledgement_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"))
| eval ack_delay_sec=ack_epoch-created_epoch
| eval step_timeout=coalesce(escalation_policy_step_timeout_sec, 600)
| eval missed_step=if(isnull(ack_epoch) OR ack_delay_sec > step_timeout,1,0)
| bin _time span=1d
| stats count as inc, sum(missed_step) as missed by _time, escalation_policy, service_name
| eval timeout_rate=round(100*missed/nullif(inc,0),2)
| where timeout_rate > 15 AND inc >= 5
| sort -timeout_rate
description: Escalation policies encode contractual response times; when acknowledgements consistently arrive after configured step timeouts, teams burn trust in paging and auto-escalations become noise. Measuring timeout_rate per policy surfaces rotation and training gaps.
value: Improves MTTA for customer-impacting services and validates on-call process investments.
implementation: Map `step_timeout` from exported escalation policy JSON if not on each incident. Treat `missed` carefully for low-urgency incidents. Pair with schedule coverage UCs for root cause.
visualization: Line chart (timeout_rate by policy), stacked bar (missed vs met), table of worst services.
grandmaExplanation: We measure how often incidents are not acknowledged within the time your playbook allows. If that happens a lot, the escalation ladder is not matching real life.
